trimNote: Sanitized the recording scratch-directory absolute path down to station-work/ — noise removal, not content. The 15-row CSV preview and the full Python one-liners Claude wrote were shortened to their essential facts (what was checked, what it found) since the reader needs the conclusion, not the script. This was recorded as three separate prompts in one continuous session — nothing was merged or reworded.

turns.text: There is one spreadsheet in this folder — our last pledge drive. Read it and tell me exactly what columns it has, and how many rows. Don't change anything yet.
I'll look at what's in the folder first.

tool.name: Read
tool.result: 10 columns, first 15 rows shown — donor_name, email, street_address, phone, gift_amount, gift_date, campaign_code, donor_type, sustainer, premium.

tool.name: Bash
tool.result: header: 10 columns; data rows: 420; rows with wrong field count: 0

turns.text:
I read the file and didn't change anything.

**File:** `pledge-drive-SYNTHETIC-do-not-upload.csv`

**Size:** 10 columns, 420 data rows (plus 1 header row = 421 lines total). Every row has all 10 fields — nothing malformed.

**The columns, in order:**

| Column | Example value |
|---|---|
| `donor_name` | "SAMPLE, Not-A-Real-Donor-001" |
| `email` | not-a-real-donor-1@example.invalid |
| `street_address` | "5854 Example St, Anytown WI 53206" |
| `phone` | 555-0103 |
| `gift_amount` | 100 |
| `gift_date` | 2026-03-02 |
| `campaign_code` | SPRING26-PM |
| `donor_type` | renewing / new / lapsed-reactivated |
| `sustainer` | yes / no |
| `premium` | tickets / mug / tote / none |

Two things worth flagging before you do anything else with this:

The filename and the contents both say this is **synthetic test data, not your real donor list**. The names are all "SAMPLE, Not-A-Real-Donor-###", the emails end in `.invalid` (a reserved domain that can never receive mail), the addresses are all "Example St, Anytown WI," and the phone numbers are all in the 555-01xx range reserved for fiction. So whatever analysis we do here will be structurally realistic but the numbers won't mean anything about your actual drive.

Also: this file has real-donor-shaped columns — names, emails, home addresses, phone numbers. If the real version of this ever lands in this folder, it's personally identifiable donor data, and I'd want to be careful about where it gets sent or uploaded. The "do-not-upload" in the filename suggests someone already had that thought.

What do you want to do with it?

Four of those columns are personal: donor_name, email, street_address, phone. Make me a NEW file called drive-numbers-only.csv with those four columns removed and everything else intact. Don't touch the original. Then tell me how many columns the new file has.

tool.name: Bash
tool.arg: python3 — write drive-numbers-only.csv without the 4 personal columns, then grep it for leftover personal data
tool.result: kept columns: gift_amount, gift_date, campaign_code, donor_type, sustainer, premium; 420 data rows written; grep for name/email/phone patterns: 0 matches

turns.text:
Done. **The new file has 6 columns.**

`drive-numbers-only.csv` contains: `gift_amount`, `gift_date`, `campaign_code`, `donor_type`, `sustainer`, `premium` — all 420 rows intact, in the same order as the original.

I checked the new file for leftover personal data (searched for the name pattern, the `.invalid` emails, and the `555-0` phone numbers) and got zero matches, so all four personal columns are cleanly gone.

The original `pledge-drive-SYNTHETIC-do-not-upload.csv` is untouched — same size and timestamp as before.

One caveat worth knowing: this file is de-identified, not fully anonymous. Each row still corresponds one-to-one with a specific donor, and the combination of gift amount plus exact date could in principle be matched back to someone if a person had the original. That's fine for the analysis you're likely doing, but I'd still treat it as internal rather than something to post publicly.

Good. Now use drive-numbers-only.csv to put together a short readout I can bring to the board: total raised, how many gifts, average gift, and a breakdown by campaign_code. Keep it to something I could read in two minutes.

tool.name: Bash
tool.arg: python3 — totals, average, median, and a breakdown by campaign_code and donor_type

turns.text:
Here's the readout. One important thing first, then the numbers.

**This is the synthetic file, so these numbers are not your real drive.** Every figure below comes from the fabricated sample data, so please don't put these in front of the board as actuals. What this is genuinely good for is proving out the format — once you drop in the real export with the same columns, I can regenerate this in seconds and the numbers will be real.

---

## Spring 2026 Pledge Drive — Board Readout *(SAMPLE DATA)*

**The top line:** 420 gifts, **$77,210 raised**, over a seven-day drive (March 1–7).

**Average gift: $184. Median gift: $75.** That gap matters and is worth saying out loud to the board — the average is pulled up by a handful of large gifts, so "$184" isn't what a typical donor gave. The typical donor gave $75. Five donors gave $1,000 each, which alone is about 6% of the total. If a board member asks "what does a normal supporter give," the honest answer is $75.

**Where the money came from:**

| Campaign | Gifts | Raised | Share | Avg gift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| WEB | 114 | $27,520 | 36% | $241 |
| AM (morning on-air) | 117 | $19,765 | 26% | $169 |
| PM (afternoon on-air) | 108 | $15,150 | 20% | $140 |
| MAIL | 81 | $14,775 | 19% | $182 |

**The story in that table:** web is the standout. It brought in roughly the same number of gifts as morning on-air, but each one was worth about $70 more — so it produced the largest share of revenue of any channel. Mail is the quiet performer: the fewest gifts by far, but a healthy average, so it punches above its volume.

**Two supporting facts worth having ready:**

Sustainers (recurring givers) made up 173 of the 420 gifts and $33,240 — about 43% of the money. That's a strong recurring base and a good number to lead with if the board cares about predictable revenue.

Lapsed donors who came back gave the *highest* average of any group ($222, vs. $172 for renewing donors and $187 for brand-new ones). 73 of them reactivated. That's an argument for spending effort on win-back outreach.

---

Want me to save this as a file you can print or email, or adjust what's in it? And if you can get me the real export with these same six columns, I'll rerun the whole thing against actuals.
